g.data-narration: Welcome to DNS - the Internet's phone book that translates names to addresses.
g.text: Name Services and the Domain Name System (DNS)

g.data-narration: DNS is a distributed hierarchical system that translates human-readable domain names like google.com into IP addresses.
g.text: Operates at Application Layer (Layer 7) | Uses UDP port 53 | Primary protocol

g.data-narration: Before DNS, a single HOSTS.TXT file managed all addresses, becoming unmanageable as the Internet grew.
The DNS namespace forms an inverted tree: root (.) at top, followed by TLDs, second-level domains, and subdomains.
When you type a domain, the recursive resolver performs a multi-step process: root → TLD → authoritative server.
The resolution goes through 4 stages: query root for TLD, query TLD for authoritative, get the final IP address.
g.text: Time: 20-80ms | Caching at each stage improves subsequent queries

g.data-narration: There are 4 main DNS server types: Root servers (13 logical), TLD servers, Authoritative servers, and Recursive resolvers.
DNS stores various record types: A for IPv4, AAAA for IPv6, CNAME for aliases, MX for mail, NS for delegation.
DNS messages have 5 sections: Header (12 bytes), Question, Answer, Authority, and Additional records.
DNS caching stores results at multiple levels: browser, OS, and recursive resolver. TTL determines cache duration, typically 300-86400 seconds.
g.text: 300s to 86400s | Lower = faster updates, more queries

g.data-narration: Recursive queries ask for complete resolution; iterative queries ask for best next step. Recursive resolvers use iterative queries internally.
g.text: "Give me the complete answer" - Server does all the work
"Give me your best answer" - Server refers to next server
Recursive resolver performs iterative queries to root → TLD → authoritative servers

g.data-narration: DNSSEC adds cryptographic signatures to DNS records, authenticating responses and preventing DNS spoofing attacks.
g.text: Authenticates DNS responses using digital signatures
DNS cache poisoning, DNS hijacking, Man-in-middle attacks
Security extensions that verify authenticity of DNS data using public-key cryptography

g.data-narration: The dig command with +trace shows the complete resolution path from root servers through TLD to the authoritative server.
Here's a practical DNS zone file for mybusiness.in with A, MX, CNAME, and NS records configured.
Avoid confusing authoritative with recursive servers, forgetting TTL impact during migration, and creating CNAME chains.
g.text: Remember: Lower MX priority number = higher preference

g.data-narration: DNS translates domain names to IP addresses, uses port 53, operates at Layer 7, and uses hierarchical distributed database design.
g.text: DNS: Hierarchical distributed database | Port 53 (UDP/TCP) | Layer 7 Application | 13 Root servers | A=IPv4, AAAA=IPv6, CNAME=Alias
